llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces MuE, a dynamic early-exiting framework for unified vision–language sequence-to-sequence models that enables simultaneous early exit in both encoder and decoder based on layer-wise similarity (saturation) rather than confidence classifiers. Proposes modality decomposition of the early-fusion encoder into tied image-encoder and text-encoder branches to allow per-modality exiting. Adds a layer-wise task loss at every decoder layer to make intermediate representations predictive and reduce performance loss. Defines an expected time reduction rate metric for seq2seq models. Achieves up to ~50% (SNLI-VE) and ~40% (MS COCO) expected time reduction while retaining ~99% and ~96% of performance, respectively; code integrated into OFA repository.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Base model: OFA (seq2seq VLM) with 6-layer encoder and 6-layer decoder.
- Modality decomposition: Duplicate/tie encoder parameters to process image tokens and text tokens separately; outputs are concatenated before the decoder, enabling independent early exit per modality.
- Early-exit signal: Use cosine similarity between consecutive layer hidden states to detect saturation and trigger exits in image encoder, text encoder, and decoder (per decoding step). For generation, apply a time-decayed threshold schedule Θ(t)=βθ+(1−β)e^{−τt/N}.
- Loss: Layer-wise cross-entropy applied to outputs from every decoder layer (averaged) to encourage informative intermediate predictions and mitigate autoregressive error accumulation.
- Decision procedure: If similarity exceeds a preset threshold for a component, skip remaining layers of that component (algorithmic pseudocode provided). Thresholds can differ across image encoder, text encoder, and decoder.
- Efficiency metric: Expected time reduction rate accounting for used encoder/decoder layers and per-step decoder exits.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Tasks/Datasets: SNLI-VE (visual entailment; accuracy reported) and MS COCO (image captioning; Karpathy split; BLEU-4, METEOR, CIDEr, SPICE; additional BLEU-1/2/3 and ROUGE-L in appendix).
- Baselines: OFA-Base, OFA-Tiny, DeeBERT, PABEE; DeeCap (decoder-only early exit) for captioning.
- Implementation: PyTorch on 4×RTX 6000 GPUs. OFA-Base backbone (hidden size 768; 6 enc/6 dec layers). Training: lr 3e-5, batch size 64, 5 epochs, Adam, weight decay 0.01. Captioning trained in two stages (XE then CIDEr optimization).
- Inference thresholds: Image encoder 0.90, text encoder 0.95; decoder 0.95 for SNLI-VE. Captioning uses decay with θ=0.99, β=0.95, τ=1.
- Results: SNLI-VE—MuE achieves ~−50% expected time with 88.5% vs 89.2% (OFA-Base), outperforming DeeBERT/PABEE in trade-off. MS COCO—~−40.2% time with BLEU-4 41.6, METEOR 30.6, CIDEr 137, SPICE 24.4 vs OFA-Base (42.8/31.7/146.7/25.8), surpassing DeeBERT/PABEE/DeeCap at similar reductions.
- Ablations: Removing modality decomposition or layer-wise task loss harms performance and/or achievable reduction (especially task loss for captioning). Modality-importance analysis shows text more crucial for SNLI-VE, image for captioning.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Performance degrades at very aggressive reduction (e.g., ~50%) on image captioning due to autoregressive difficulty; captioning appears to require more layers to maintain quality.
- Slight performance drop at zero reduction likely from training with decomposition strategy.
- Fixed similarity thresholds are heuristic; not learned or policy-optimized; may require task-specific tuning.
- Evaluation limited to two datasets (SNLI-VE, MS COCO) and one backbone (OFA); generalization to other models/tasks not empirically demonstrated.
- Assumes saturation behavior holds broadly across layers and modalities; may vary by architecture/data.
- Expected time reduction is a proxy metric; real wall-clock speedups can vary by hardware/runtime.
- Interaction/dependency between encoder and decoder exits handled heuristically; joint optimal scheduling not explored.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Learn exit policies/thresholds via reinforcement learning or differentiable gating, possibly conditioned on modality and instance difficulty.
- Token-level dynamic computation (e.g., skipping/pruning image tokens or text tokens) as an alternative to layer skipping, especially for captioning.
- Combine MuE with complementary efficiency methods (quantization, distillation, pruning, token sparsification) for compounded gains.
- Extend evaluation to more VLMs (e.g., BLIP, SimVLM, CoCa) and tasks (VQA, VRD, retrieval, grounded captioning) to test generality.
- Hardware-aware and batch-aware scheduling for practical latency/throughput optimization; integrate with compilers/accelerators.
- Adaptive per-modality and per-layer thresholding based on confidence/uncertainty estimates, or hybrid signals (similarity + entropy).
- Theoretical analysis of saturation dynamics in multi-modal transformers to formalize exit guarantees; calibration techniques for early exits.
- Robustness and safety: study effects on rare/long-tail examples, OOD inputs, and error propagation in generation; add consistency constraints across exits.

research_study_list.title: A Simple Romance Between Multi-Exit Vision Transformer and Token Reduction

new_method.method:
Title: MoD-RCC — Risk-Controlled, Value-Aware Mixture-of-Depths for Token-Wise Dynamic Computation

Motivation
- Problem: Mixture-of-Depths (MoD) saves FLOPs by routing only some tokens deeper, but current routers use ad-hoc proxies (e.g., confidence/entropy) that (i) are misaligned with end-task loss, (ii) lack user-settable guarantees on degradation, (iii) ignore complementary roles of shallow vs deep computation, and (iv) misallocate compute under distribution shift.
- Gap: No method estimates per-token marginal value of extra depth in loss units, optimizes value-per-FLOP under a runtime budget, and provides distribution-free guarantees on degradation at the sample level. Existing risk-control (Fast yet Safe) addresses layer-wise exits, not spatial/token routing, and lacks value-aware allocation; CODA offers practical token routing but without loss-aligned value signals or guarantees; DAT targets decoder exits, not encoder-side MoD.
- Why now: Token-conditional computation is production-ready; distribution-free post-hoc calibration is mature; efficient sparse attention and gather/scatter kernels exist. Bringing these together enables safe, controllable acceleration for latency/energy-constrained vision and 3D workloads.

Methodology
High-level system: Equip a ViT-like MoD backbone with four modules—(A) a calibrated twin-critic that predicts per-token marginal compute value (MCV) in task-loss units; (B) a budgeted soft top-k router that maximizes value-per-FLOP with fairness/coverage; (C) shallow–deep complementary heads with uncertainty-aware fusion; (D) a post-hoc, distribution-free risk calibrator that maps predicted per-sample degradation into expectation/high-probability guarantees. Inference knobs: compute budget B and risk cap ε (optional δ for high-probability).

A) Twin-Critic for marginal compute value (loss-to-go)
- Objective: For token i at layer l, predict V(i,l) ≈ E[ℓshallow − ℓdeeper | x,i,l], i.e., expected loss reduction if routed Δl deeper.
- Counterfactual supervision: Per batch, sample a small token subset; for each, run both paths (stop at l vs continue l→l+Δl) to obtain Δℓcf; sample Δl∈{1..Δmax} stochastically; use importance weights for unbiasedness.
- TD bootstrapping: Learn a temporal-difference critic over depth with Bellman consistency V(i,l) ≈ r(i,l,Δl) + γV(i,l+Δl); anchor to counterfactual labels when available (L2).
- Doubly-robust off-policy estimator: Combine TD predictions and counterfactuals with importance weights from an exploration router (Gumbel-Top-k) to correct policy mismatch and reduce variance.
- Uncertainty and calibration: Predict mean μ and aleatoric σ via heteroscedastic regression; fit an isotonic map from μ to realized Δℓcf on a held-out split to enforce monotonicity. Use risk-adjusted value Ṽ = μ − η·σ, where η is learned via a Lagrangian to satisfy a target underestimation rate.
- Novelty: Directly learns loss-to-go at token-depth granularity (encoder-side). Contrasts with CODA’s difficulty proxies and with DAT’s decoder exits. Produces calibrated, uncertainty-aware value signals rather than raw confidence.

B) Budgeted, fairness-aware soft top-k routing
- Optimization: At each layer l, select tokens S_l maximizing ∑_{i∈S_l} (Ṽ(i,l) − λ·c_l), where c_l is marginal FLOPs/latency, s.t. |S_l| ≤ k_l and optional group quotas.
- Differentiable router: Entropy-regularized soft top-k yields continuous selection weights during training; hard top-k at inference. Share light FiLM parameters across layers to limit overhead.
- Dual budget control: Update λ by dual ascent to meet a runtime FLOPs/latency budget B; can be re-tuned online without retraining. Optionally use hardware-aware costs c_l measured on the target device (latency tables) rather than FLOPs.
- Fairness/coverage constraints: Add diversity/coverage regularizers (e.g., DPP-style over spatial cells; strided or grid quotas) to avoid starving regions and improve stability under shift.
- Novelty: First coupling of per-token value-per-FLOP maximization with online dual budget control and fairness for MoD; prior routers optimize proxy importance without cost-aware value.

C) Shallow–deep complementary prediction with uncertainty fusion
- Head design: Provide shallow heads for early-stopped tokens and deep heads for routed tokens. For dense tasks, decompose predictions into low/high-frequency subbands (e.g., Laplacian pyramid) and fuse by uncertainty-aware weights.
- Complementarity learning: Encourage low correlation and opposite-signed residuals via HSIC penalty and tanh-smoothed sign-decorrelation; project deep residuals onto the orthogonal complement of shallow features (spectral regularizer) for diversity and stability.
- Fusion: Weight subbands and heads by estimated aleatoric uncertainty to avoid error amplification in early exits.
- Novelty: Complementarity along computation depth (shallow vs deep) targeted to MoD; distinct from MonoCD’s cue-wise complementarity.

D) Distribution-free risk calibration and guarantees
- Per-sample degradation proxy: Aggregate token-level values to predict a sample-level Δℓ̂ (sum predicted gains for routed tokens minus penalties for skipped ones; average over pixels for segmentation).
- Post-hoc isotonic calibration: Fit a monotone map from Δℓ̂ to observed Δℓ = ℓexit − ℓfull on a small calibration set.
- Guarantees: Select a threshold θ on Δℓ̂ to satisfy user caps.
  • Expectation control: CRC-style selection ensures E[Δℓ] ≤ ε under bounded loss and monotonicity.
  • High-probability control: Waudby-Smith–Ramdas UCB adapted to losses in [−B,B] (allowing negative Δℓ from overthinking credit) to guarantee P(Δℓ ≤ ε) ≥ 1 − δ. Apply at image/scene level; extend to mean-pixel Brier for dense outputs.
- Streaming/shift robustness: Optional online, anytime-valid updates of θ via WSR to maintain guarantees under mild distribution drift.
- Novelty vs Fast yet Safe: Extends from layer exits to spatial token routing with calibrated value aggregation and both expectation/high-probability guarantees.

Training and recipe
- Stage 0 (Dense reference): Pretrain/finetune the full-depth backbone and task heads to serve as the reference model for Δℓ.
- Stage 1 (Twin-Critic): Train the twin-critic with counterfactual token sampling (few tokens per batch), TD consistency, and doubly-robust loss; then isotonic-calibrate μ→Δℓ on a validation split.
- Stage 2 (Router + heads): Jointly train router and complementary heads with dual budget control and diversity/fairness regularizers; use off-policy corrections where tokens deviate from the exploration policy.
- Stage 3 (Risk calibrator): Post-hoc fit isotonic Δℓ̂→Δℓ, select θ for expectation or high-probability control using the chosen ε (and δ), and store mappings for inference.
- Runtime engineering: Packed gather/scatter for selected tokens; block-sparse or FlashAttention kernels; pre-allocate buffers for max k_l; device-specific latency tables for c_l; amortize soft top-k solver cost by sharing router parameters and low-iteration updates.

Code interfaces
- TwinCriticMCV(l), SoftTopKRouter(k_l, FiLM), BudgetController(dual λ with hardware-aware costs), ComplementaryHeads (shallow/deep + subbands), UncertaintyFusion, RiskCalibrator (CRC + WSR), DynamicTokenPacker.
- Inference API: predict(x, budget=B, epsilon=ε, mode={expectation|high-prob}), returning outputs, used FLOPs/latency, and rolling empirical risk.

How this differs from prior work
- CODA: We learn calibrated loss-to-go and optimize value-per-FLOP with dual budget control and fairness; CODA routes by proxy difficulty without loss-aligned value or risk guarantees.
- DAT: Decoder-side early exits vs our encoder-side token-depth routing with guarantees and complementarity; no distribution-free control in DAT.
- Fast yet Safe: Extends distribution-free control from global early exits to spatial token routing via calibrated value aggregation; supports negative-loss credit and dense tasks.
- Task models (MonoCD/HRDFuse/DeCoTR/MixNeRF/DynamicStereo/WorDepth): Focus on task prediction; MoD-RCC is orthogonal—optimizes compute allocation with guarantees and depth-wise complementarity and can be plugged into these backbones.

Evaluation plan
- Tasks/datasets: ImageNet (classification), Cityscapes (segmentation), NYU/KITTI (monocular depth), optional NeRF (PSNR-based Δℓ on LLFF/DTU).
- Metrics: Pareto fronts (FLOPs/latency vs task metric), risk satisfaction vs ε (and δ), OOD stability on corruption benchmarks (ImageNet-C/Cityscapes-C), MCV ranking AUC (value–gain rank correlation), value leakage rate (missed top-value tokens).
- Ablations: critic variants (TD-only vs DR), uncertainty calibration, fairness regularizers, hardware-aware costs vs FLOPs, shallow–deep complementarity penalties, online vs offline risk calibration.

Expected impact
- Safe, controllable acceleration: Users set ε (and δ) to cap degradation relative to full depth while dialing compute budget B; suitable for edge/real-time deployments.
- Better compute allocation: Calibrated loss-to-go concentrates depth on tokens that matter, improving Pareto efficiency over uncertainty/difficulty heuristics.
- Robust, smooth degradation: Depth-wise complementarity and uncertainty fusion mitigate quality cliffs; post-hoc risk control provides predictable behavior under shift.
- Broad applicability: Drop-in for ViT/SegFormer/HRNet; extends to 3D and NeRF by treating ray samples as tokens and using PSNR-based Δℓ; hardware-aware costs enable practical latency control.
- Reusable assets: Open-source modules for value-aware routing with guarantees, per-token loss-to-go datasets, and standardized evaluation for risk–FLOPs/latency trade-offs.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Experiment 1: Value-aware MoD with risk control on image classification
Goal/Hypotheses
- H1: Twin-Critic MCV improves compute allocation over proxy routers, yielding a better FLOPs/latency–accuracy Pareto.
- H2: Distribution-free risk calibration enforces user-chosen degradation caps ε in expectation and high probability.

Setup
- Dataset: ImageNet-100 (100-class subset of ImageNet-1k) or ImageNet-1k with pretrained weights (timm) to keep training feasible.
- Backbone: ViT-S/16 or DeiT-S (timm). Treat 14x14 patch tokens as routing units.
- Loss: Cross-entropy. Full-depth model serves as reference for Δℓ.
- Hardware-aware costs: Measure per-layer latency c_l via torch.cuda.Event timers or torch.utils.benchmark.
- Baselines: (a) Full model (upper bound). (b) Proxy router: token entropy/variance or attention magnitude (CODA-like). (c) Uniform top-k routing. (d) Layer-wise early exit (Fast yet Safe) with similar latency.

Implementation highlights
- Stage 0: Finetune/full-depth reference for 30–50 epochs (if using pretrained, 10–20 epochs suffice). Save logits and attention maps hooks.
- Stage 1: Train Twin-Critic MCV
  • Critic head: small MLP over per-token features [token embedding, layer id one-hot, local attention stats, shallow head logits]; outputs mean μ and log-σ.
  • Counterfactual labels: per batch, sample S≈16 tokens across images; sample Δl∈{1,2,3} uniformly; run shallow stop at l and deeper l→l+Δl; compute Δℓ_cf = ℓ_shallow − ℓ_deeper.
  • Loss: doubly-robust L2 using Gumbel-Top-k exploration policy π_e with importance weights w = π_t/π_e; TD term with V(i,l) ≈ r(i,l,Δl)+γV(i,l+Δl).
  • Calibration: isotonic regression on held-out val split mapping μ→Δℓ_cf; risk-adjusted Ṽ = μ − η·σ (η via Lagrangian to maintain underestimation rate ≤ 5%).
- Stage 2: Router + budget control
  • SoftTopKRouter per layer with entropy regularization; objective sum_i (Ṽ(i,l) − λ·c_l); hard top-k at inference. Dual-ascent update on λ to hit target budget B.
  • Train with complementary shallow/deep heads minimally (classification: shallow head predicts class using shallow tokens only; deep head for routed tokens). Loss = CE(fused logits, y) + DR loss consistency.
- Stage 3: Risk calibrator
  • Aggregate per-token values to sample-level Δℓ̂ (sum of routed token Ṽ minus penalty for skipped top-V tokens). Fit isotonic Δℓ̂→Δℓ on a 5–10k image calibration set.
  • For expectation control: choose θ so mean observed Δℓ ≤ ε on calib.
  • For high-prob: Waudby-Smith–Ramdas anytime-valid bound to pick θ with P(Δℓ ≤ ε) ≥ 1−δ.

Evaluation
- Pareto: plot FLOPs or measured latency vs Top-1 accuracy across B and ε; report area-under-Pareto (AUP).
- Risk satisfaction: grid ε∈{0.2,0.5,1.0} top-1 drop; measure E[Δℓ] and violation rate 1{Δℓ>ε}; for HP guarantee, report empirical P(Δℓ ≤ ε) and Clopper–Pearson CI.
- Value quality: MCV ranking AUC (Spearman/Kendall between predicted Ṽ and realized Δℓ_cf on probed tokens); value leakage rate (fraction of top-α% true-value tokens not routed).
- OOD robustness: ImageNet-C severity 1–5; repeat risk satisfaction and Pareto; also test with online WSR adaptation vs static θ.
- Ablations: TD-only vs DR; with/without isotonic; risk-adjusted Ṽ with η vs plain μ; hardware-aware c_l vs FLOPs.

Feasibility and expected runtime
- With ViT-S and ImageNet-100: full recipe in 1–2 days on 2×A100 (or 3090-class GPUs). Counterfactual probing adds ~10–20% overhead due to sparse token sampling.

Minimal Python sketch (pseudo)

import torch, random
from sklearn.isotonic import IsotonicRegression

# counterfactual probing inside training
for x, y in loader:
    feats = backbone.encode_until_all_layers(x)  # cache per-layer token embeddings
    losses_shallow, losses_deeper, V_targets, iw = [], [], [], []
    for b in range(x.size(0)):
        for (i,l) in sample_tokens_layers(feats[b], S=16):
            d = random.choice([1,2,3])
            loss_sh = loss_fn(head_shallow(feats[b][:, :l]), y[b])
            loss_dp = loss_fn(head_deep(run_deeper(feats[b], l, d)), y[b])
            V_targets.append((loss_sh - loss_dp).detach())
            iw.append(importance_weight(router, i, l, d))
    mu, log_sigma = critic(token_feats)  # batched
    dr_loss = doubly_robust_l2(mu, log_sigma, V_targets, iw) + td_consistency(mu)
    router_loss = -(value_minus_cost(mu, log_sigma, c_l, lambda_dual)).mean()
    task_loss = loss_fn(fuse(head_shallow, head_deep), y)
    (task_loss + dr_loss + router_reg).backward(); opt.step()

# isotonic calibration of critic
iso = IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds='clip').fit(mu_val, V_targets_val)

# budget control
lambda_dual += rho * (measured_latency - target_latency)

# inference with risk control
Delta_hat = aggregate_token_values(V_tilde, routed_tokens)
if mode == 'expectation':
    use_exit = (Delta_hat >= theta_eps)
elif mode == 'high-prob':
    use_exit = wsr_accept(Delta_hat, epsilon, delta)



Experiment 2: Dense segmentation with shallow–deep complementarity and fairness
Goal/Hypotheses
- H3: Complementary shallow–deep heads with uncertainty fusion mitigate quality cliffs and improve Pareto vs single-head routing.
- H4: Fairness/coverage constraints reduce spatial starvation and improve OOD stability.

Setup
- Dataset: Cityscapes (fine labels), optional Cityscapes-C for OOD (corruptions).
- Backbone: SegFormer-B0/SegFormer-B2 (Mit-B0/B2 encoder) or ViT-S with simple FPN head.
- Tokens: patch tokens at 1/4 or 1/8 stride.
- Loss: mean pixel-wise cross-entropy + auxiliary dice (optional).

Implementation highlights
- Stage 0: Train full-depth reference to target mIoU.
- Stage 1: Twin-Critic as in Exp1 but Δℓ_cf computed as per-pixel cross-entropy averaged over the image; token-probing selects spatial tokens; Δl∈{1,2} typically enough for small backbones.
- Stage 2: Router + complementary heads
  • Shallow head predicts low-frequency logits (e.g., after average pooling or Laplacian pyramid low band). Deep head predicts residual high-frequency logits only.
  • Loss: CE(fuse(shallow, deep), y) + λ_hsic HSIC(shallow_feat, deep_resid) + λ_spec spectral-orthogonalizer to keep deep residual in orthogonal complement of shallow features.
  • Uncertainty fusion: predict per-pixel aleatoric σ_sh, σ_dp; fuse logits with weights ∝ 1/σ^2.
  • Fairness: add DPP-style diversity or grid quotas per 8x8 spatial cells; mild entropy regularization on router.
- Stage 3: Risk calibrator at sample or per-pixel averaged level; set θ for ε in mIoU drop measured as Δℓ (negative if improved).

Evaluation
- Pareto: GFLOPs/latency vs mIoU; report gains at equal budget and at equal mIoU.
- Risk satisfaction: E[Δℓ] and high-prob guarantee on Cityscapes val/test; then evaluate on Cityscapes-C to assess robustness and effect of online adaptation.
- Spatial coverage: fraction of frames with any empty 8x8 cell, Jensen-Shannon between routing heatmap and uniform grid, and correlation with semantic boundaries.
- Ablations: without complementarity penalties; without uncertainty fusion; without fairness; proxy vs MCV router.
- Visuals: overlay routed tokens per layer to inspect coverage and boundary focus.

Feasibility and expected runtime
- B0/B2 variants feasible on a single 24GB GPU; 1–2 days total with probing.

Minimal Python sketch (pseudo)

# Complementarity losses
def hsic(x, y):
    # unbiased HSIC estimate with RBF kernels
    ...

def spectral_orthogonalize(resid, shallow_feat):
    # project resid onto orthogonal complement via small bottleneck
    ...

logits_sh, sigma_sh = head_shallow(features_sh)
logits_dp, sigma_dp = head_deep(features_dp)
logits_fused = (logits_sh/sigma_sh**2 + logits_dp/sigma_dp**2) / (1/sigma_sh**2 + 1/sigma_dp**2)

loss = ce_loss(logits_fused, y) + lam_hsic*hsic(feat_sh, logits_dp) + lam_spec*spectral_orthogonalize(logits_dp, feat_sh)

# Fairness quota per grid cell
topk_mask = router(features, k_l)
quota_penalty = sum(max(0, tokens_in_cell(cell) - q_cell) for cell in grid)
(loss + alpha*quota_penalty).backward()



Experiment 3: Online distribution-free risk control under shift (streaming ImageNet-C/Cityscapes-C)
Goal/Hypotheses
- H5: Post-hoc isotonic + WSR high-prob calibrator maintains risk caps under distribution shift with anytime-valid updates, unlike static thresholds or proxy routers.

Setup
- Stream: concatenate corruption severities 1→5 (ImageNet-C) or Cityscapes-C across corruption types; process in order to simulate drift.
- Use MoD-RCC from Exp1/Exp2 with fixed budget B.
- Modes: (a) Static θ from in-domain calibration. (b) Online WSR with alpha-spending; update θ after each mini-batch of 64 samples using nonparametric UCB on Δℓ.
- Baselines: proxy router with CRC-like static calibration; Early-exit FYS.

Procedure
- Initialize θ0 from clean validation. For t=1..T samples in stream:
  • Run inference to get Δℓ̂_t and observed Δℓ_t (vs full-depth reference precomputed or computed asynchronously on a second GPU/CPU with smaller batch).
  • Decide accept if Δℓ̂_t ≥ θ_t; otherwise force full compute (to estimate realized Δℓ if needed) or log deferral count.
  • Update θ_{t+1} using WSR supermartingale-based UCB with bounded losses in [−B, B]. Keep a rolling window variant for faster adaptation (optional) but report anytime-valid guarantee on full history.

Metrics
- Guarantee tracking: empirical violation rate sup over prefixes, sup_t (1/t)∑1{Δℓ_s>ε}; maintain ≤ δ with 95% CI.
- Regret vs static: difference in average latency and average Δℓ; measure compute saved while satisfying guarantees.
- Robustness to abrupt shifts: shuffle corruption types; add brightness shift not in calibration; report time-to-recover (samples to regain guarantee after shift).

Feasibility and expected runtime
- Streaming evaluation only; no extra training. Reference model runs can be amortized via offline precomputation on a subset or asynchronous worker.

Minimal Python sketch (pseudo)

from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint

S_upper = 0.0  # WSR supermartingale statistic placeholder
for t, (x, y) in enumerate(stream):
    out, Delta_hat = mod_rcc.predict(x, budget=B)
    if Delta_hat >= theta:
        use_exit = True
    else:
        use_exit = False
    Delta = measure_degradation(x, y, out, full_ref)  # async or cached
    violate = 1.0 if Delta > epsilon else 0.0
    S_upper = wsr_update(S_upper, violate, delta)
    theta = update_threshold(theta, S_upper)
    viol_rate = running_mean(violate)
    lo, hi = proportion_confint(int(viol_sum), t+1, method='beta')


General notes for all experiments
- Practical kernels: use torch.compile or xformers/FlashAttention2; gather/scatter packed tensors for routed tokens; preallocate buffers sized to max k_l per layer.
- Hardware-aware costs: build per-layer latency tables by sweeping token counts on the target GPU; plug into c_l.
- Reproducibility: fix seeds; log-routing decisions and Δℓ via lightweight parquet.
- Hyperparameter suggestions: γ=0.9 in TD; η learned via dual with target 5% underestimation; dual step rho=1e-3; soft-topk temperature 0.5; fairness grid 8x8 with quota 2–4 tokens/cell depending on k_l.
- Success criteria: consistent Pareto improvement over baselines, calibrated MCV with high rank correlation, and empirical satisfaction of risk caps both in-domain and under shift with online WSR.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
MoD-RCC — Comprehensive Experimental Plan and Implementation Details (PyTorch)

This document elaborates the experiments in the strategy, provides concrete implementation details, reliable evaluation procedures, and example code sketches using PyTorch and widely used Python libraries. The plan is organized by experiment and integrates shared infrastructure to avoid redundancy.

Shared infrastructure and modules (used by all experiments)
- Framework and libs: PyTorch (torch, torchvision), timm (models), xFormers/FlashAttention2 (attention kernels), scikit-learn (isotonic regression), torchmetrics (metrics), scipy (stats), pandas/pyarrow (logging), torch.utils.benchmark (latency), Weights & Biases or TensorBoard (logging), Hydra or OmegaConf (config), numpy/random (seeding), einops (tensor reshaping), kornia (image transforms and Laplacian pyramid), tqdm (progress).
- Backbone modification: Wrap a ViT-like encoder to expose per-layer token embeddings, per-layer attention stats, and to support token-wise routing via gather/scatter. Use xformers.ops.memory_efficient_attention or flash_attn for speed.
- Tokens: The standard ViT patch tokens after patch embedding; CLS token handled specially (never pruned or always routed shallow-deep consistently). For segmentation, use spatial patch tokens (no CLS).
- DynamicTokenPacker: Packs selected tokens per layer into contiguous buffers to run deeper blocks only on routed tokens. Maintain inverse scatter indices to restore spatial order. Pre-allocate per-layer maximum capacity buffers to avoid dynamic CUDA allocs.
- Hardware-aware latency tables: Build per-layer cost table c_l(tokens) using torch.cuda.Event and torch.utils.benchmark.Timer by sweeping token counts. Cache table and interpolate at runtime.
- Random seeds: seed everything (torch, numpy, random); enable deterministic algorithms where feasible, with caution around flash-attn kernels. Log seeds.
- Losses: CrossEntropyLoss for classification; pixel-wise CE (+ optional Dice) for segmentation. All Δℓ are computed as task-loss differences vs a full-depth reference run, per sample.
- FLOPs/latency reporting: Prefer measured latency via timers over FLOPs when optimizing c_l. Report both.
- Calibration splits: Reserve 5–10k samples for calibration and not for training. Optionally use 2-fold calibration (fit on one half, validate on the other) to detect overfitting of the calibrator.

Core MoD-RCC modules
- TwinCriticMCV: predicts per-token marginal compute value V(i,l) in loss units, with heteroscedastic regression (μ, σ).
- SoftTopKRouter: differentiable soft selection during training; hard top-k at inference. Support entropy regularization and optional grid quotas.
- BudgetController: dual variable λ to meet a runtime budget; integrates hardware-aware costs c_l.
- ComplementaryHeads + UncertaintyFusion: shallow and deep heads; uncertainty-weighted fusion; optional Laplacian pyramid for dense tasks.
- RiskCalibrator: isotonic regression mapping Δℓ̂→Δℓ and threshold selection for expectation control (CRC-style) and high-probability control (WSR-style updates for streams).

Example code: module skeletons (abbreviated)

class TwinCriticMCV(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_layers, hidden=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.layer_embed = torch.nn.Embedding(n_layers, 32)
        self.mlp = torch.nn.Sequential(
            torch.nn.Linear(d_model + 32 + 16, hidden),
            torch.nn.ReLU(),
            torch.nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            torch.nn.ReLU(),
        )
        self.head_mu = torch.nn.Linear(hidden, 1)
        self.head_logsigma = torch.nn.Linear(hidden, 1)
    def forward(self, token_embed, layer_id, aux):
        # token_embed: [N_tok, d_model], layer_id: [N_tok], aux: [N_tok, 16]
        lid = self.layer_embed(layer_id)
        h = self.mlp(torch.cat([token_embed, lid, aux], dim=-1))
        mu = self.head_mu(h).squeeze(-1)
        log_sigma = self.head_logsigma(h).squeeze(-1).clamp(-6, 3)
        return mu, log_sigma

class SoftTopKRouter(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, k_per_layer, temperature=0.5, entropy_reg=0.01):
        super().__init__()
        self.scorer = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, 1)
        self.k_per_layer = k_per_layer
        self.tau = temperature
        self.entropy_reg = entropy_reg
    def forward(self, tokens, value_signal, layer_id, grid_mask=None):
        # tokens: [N_tok, d], value_signal: [N_tok], returns weights in [0,1]
        logits = value_signal  # already cost-adjusted Ṽ - λ c_l
        # soft top-k via Gumbel + softmax sharpening
        g = -torch.log(-torch.log(torch.rand_like(logits)))
        y = (logits + g) / self.tau
        w = torch.softmax(y, dim=0)
        # encourage sparsity towards top-k
        k = self.k_per_layer
        # scale so sum w ≈ k; stop grad on k
        w = w * (k / (w.sum() + 1e-6))
        if grid_mask is not None:
            w = w * grid_mask  # zero out masked cells if using quotas
        # optional entropy regularization value: -(w*log w).sum()
        return w
    def hard_topk(self, logits):
        k = self.k_per_layer
        idx = torch.topk(logits, k, dim=0).indices
        mask = torch.zeros_like(logits, dtype=torch.bool)
        mask[idx] = True
        return mask

class BudgetController:
    def __init__(self, target_latency_ms, rho=1e-3):
        self.lmbda = torch.tensor(0.0, device='cuda')
        self.target_latency = target_latency_ms
        self.rho = rho
    def update(self, measured_latency_ms):
        self.lmbda = (self.lmbda + self.rho * (measured_latency_ms - self.target_latency)).clamp(min=0.0)
        return self.lmbda

# Isotonic calibrator and threshold selection
from sklearn.isotonic import IsotonicRegression
class RiskCalibrator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.iso = IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds='clip')
        self.theta = None
    def fit(self, delta_hat, delta_true):
        self.iso.fit(delta_hat, delta_true)
    def pick_theta_expectation(self, delta_hat, delta_true, epsilon):
        # grid search theta so mean(Δℓ | Δℓ̂ ≥ θ) ≤ ε
        ths = torch.quantile(torch.as_tensor(delta_hat), torch.linspace(0, 1, 101)).tolist()
        best = ths[0]
        for th in ths:
            mask = (torch.as_tensor(delta_hat) >= th)
            if mask.sum() == 0: continue
            mean_deg = torch.as_tensor(delta_true)[mask].mean().item()
            if mean_deg <= epsilon: best = th
        self.theta = best
        return best
    def map(self, delta_hat):
        return self.iso.predict(delta_hat)


Experiment 1 — Value-aware MoD with risk control on ImageNet classification
Goals/Hypotheses
- H1: Twin-Critic MCV improves compute allocation over proxy routers, yielding a better FLOPs/latency–accuracy Pareto.
- H2: Distribution-free risk calibration enforces user-chosen degradation caps ε in expectation and high probability.

Dataset and preprocessing
- Dataset: ImageNet-1k with pretrained weights from timm. For quick iteration, optionally ImageNet-100 (100-class subset). Use torchvision.datasets.ImageNet or a local copy via timm loaders.
- Splits: Train (1.2M), Val (50k). From Val, set aside 10k for calibration (5k for isotonic fit, 5k for threshold selection/validation) to avoid overfitting calibrators.
- Preprocessing: Standard ImageNet transforms (random resized crop 224, horizontal flip); evaluation: center crop 224. Normalize per ImageNet statistics.

Backbone and tokens
- Model: DeiT-S/16 or ViT-S/16 from timm. Patch size 16 → 14×14 tokens + CLS = 197 tokens per image.
- Routing units: spatial tokens only; CLS always routed to full depth or treated as a separate always-deep token to stabilize classification.
- Depth routing: Enable routing decisions at selected layers, e.g., every 2nd block among 12 (layers L={2,4,6,8,10}). Δl ∈ {1,2,3}.

Reference model (Stage 0)
- Start from timm pretrained weights; finetune the full-depth model for 10–20 epochs on ImageNet-1k with standard recipe (AdamW lr=5e-5, wd=0.05, cosine decay, label smoothing 0.1, mixup 0.2, CutMix 0.2, drop path 0.1). Save the final checkpoint as the reference. Cache its per-sample full-depth loss for calibration where possible.

Twin-Critic training (Stage 1)
- Features: For each token and layer, assemble inputs: token embedding (d=384), layer one-hot/embedding, auxiliary 16-d features (local attention entropy, token L2 norm, shallow head logits for current prefix, position encodings).
- Exploration policy: Use Gumbel-Top-k router with high temperature (τ=1.0) to sample tokens for deeper paths during exploration. Log exploration probabilities π_e(i,l,Δl). Use importance weights w = π_t/π_e (π_t is current router’s probability; at the beginning set π_t=π_e or uniform to reduce variance).
- Counterfactual labels: Per batch, sample S=16 tokens per image across chosen layers; sample Δl uniformly from {1,2,3} where feasible (
   clamp to remaining depth). For each sampled token: (a) compute shallow loss if stopping at layer l (run shallow head using prefix representation); (b) compute deeper loss after routing that token Δl deeper (pack selected tokens, run Δl blocks on them, fuse deep head outputs). Δℓ_cf = ℓ_shallow − ℓ_deeper for the sample. Allow negative values.
- TD bootstrapping: Predict V(i,l). Define r(i,l,Δl) = realized immediate Δℓ_cf over Δl steps (when observed), then enforce V(i,l) ≈ r(i,l,Δl) + γ V(i,l+Δl) with γ=0.9 using MSE on predictions. When no counterfactual label is available, train purely on TD consistency by predicting both V(i,l) and V(i,l+Δl) from the same forward pass.
- Doubly robust loss: For tokens with counterfactuals, minimize (importance-weighted) heteroscedastic NLL plus DR correction term to reduce bias: L_DR = E[w · ((Δℓ_cf − μ)^2 / (2σ^2) + 0.5 log σ^2)] + β · (μ − TD_target)^2, with β≈0.5. Clip w to a max (e.g., 10) to control variance.
- Underestimation control: Add a one-sided penalty to enforce a target underestimation rate ≤5%: L_under = λ_under · mean(max(0, μ − Δℓ_cf)), and update λ_under by dual ascent to hit a desired quantile. This encourages conservative μ.
- Calibration: After Stage 1 training, fit isotonic regression on a held-out split mapping μ to Δℓ_cf to enforce monotonicity. Risk-adjust values at inference: Ṽ = iso(μ) − η·σ, learning η via a small dual loop to target ≤5% underestimation on validation.

Router and budget (Stage 2)
- Cost-aware selection: At each routing layer l, compute per-token score s(i,l) = Ṽ(i,l) − λ · c_l, where c_l is the marginal measured latency for routing that token deeper by one block (or averaged across Δl, per latency table).
- Soft selection: Train using SoftTopKRouter to produce continuous weights w(i,l) with entropy regularization (0.01–0.05). Apply straight-through hard top-k for stability if needed.
- Budget control: Maintain a latency budget B (e.g., 50%, 60%, 70% of full-depth latency). During training, for each mini-batch measure runtime latency via torch.cuda.Event or benchmark.Timer; update λ ← [λ + ρ (latency − B)]_+ with ρ=1e-3. For fast feedback, approximate latency via the precomputed table and occasionally correct with measured latency.
- Heads and fusion: For classification, implement a shallow head that produces class logits from the prefix representation (e.g., CLS token at layer l or pooled selected tokens), and a deep head that refines logits from routed tokens. Fuse logits via uncertainty weighting: logits = (logits_sh/σ_sh^2 + logits_dp/σ_dp^2) / (1/σ_sh^2 + 1/σ_dp^2). Predict log σ per head via an auxiliary linear layer trained with heteroscedastic CE (NLL with learned variance).
- Losses: L_total = CE(fused, y) + α_DR L_DR + α_TD L_TD + α_ent H(router). Typical weights: α_DR=1.0, α_TD=0.1, α_ent=1e-3.

Risk calibrator (Stage 3)
- Δℓ̂ aggregation: For each sample, aggregate token-level Ṽ for routed tokens and subtract a small penalty for skipped high-value tokens (to avoid optimism): Δℓ̂ = Σ_{i∈routed} Ṽ(i,l_i) − κ · Σ_{j∈top-M unrouted} Ṽ(j,l_j), with κ≈0.2, M≈5% of tokens.
- Fit isotonic map from Δℓ̂ to observed Δℓ = ℓ_exit − ℓ_full on the calibration set. Choose θ for user ε using expectation control: minimal θ such that mean(Δℓ | Δℓ̂ ≥ θ) ≤ ε.
- High-probability control: For streaming or batched HP guarantees, use Waudby-Smith–Ramdas style anytime-valid UCB for Bernoulli violations 1{Δℓ > ε}, updating θ online (details in Exp3).

Baselines
- Full model (upper bound latency, accuracy baseline).
- Proxy router: CODA-like: scores from token entropy of local attention, token norm variance, or attention magnitude from the last attended layer; train a soft top-k router on proxy signals without critic.
- Uniform top-k: Randomly select k tokens per layer uniformly.
- Fast yet Safe early exit: Add layer-wise exits with CRC; choose the exit threshold to match similar average latency as MoD-RCC; provides a strong non-spatial baseline.

Evaluation Protocol
- Hardware-aware Pareto: For B in a grid (e.g., 40–90% of full latency) and ε in {0.2, 0.5, 1.0} top-1 drop, sweep inference to produce (latency, top-1) pairs. Plot curves and compute AUP (area under Pareto) via trapezoidal rule.
- Risk satisfaction: On the held-out test set, report E[Δℓ] and violation rate P(Δℓ > ε). For HP mode with δ ∈ {0.05, 0.1}, report empirical P(Δℓ ≤ ε) and Clopper–Pearson 95% CI.
- Value quality: Compute Spearman and Kendall tau between Ṽ and realized Δℓ_cf on a set of 10k probed tokens sampled across images/layers. Value leakage: fraction of top-α% true-value tokens missed by the router.
- OOD robustness: Evaluate ImageNet-C (all corruption types, severity 1–5). Report Pareto curves and risk satisfaction; compare static θ vs online WSR adaptation.
- Ablations: TD-only vs DR; with/without isotonic; risk-adjusted Ṽ vs raw μ; hardware-aware c_l vs FLOPs; router with/without entropy regularization; exploration temperature.
- Statistical reliability: Bootstrap 1k resamples for AUP differences; report 95% CIs; paired t-tests or Wilcoxon for accuracy at matched latency.

Hyperparameters and runtime
- Training: Stage 1 (critic) 10–15 epochs with counterfactual probes (adds ~15% overhead), Stage 2 (router+heads) 10–15 epochs. Batch size 256 on 2×A100 or 3090s mixed precision. Total ~1–2 days.
- Probing: S=16 tokens/image, Δl in {1,2,3}, clip importance weights at 10.
- SoftTopK temperatures: start 1.0 → cosinedown to 0.4.

Example training loop snippets (abbreviated, Python)

# Counterfactual probing snippet inside the training step
for imgs, targets in train_loader:
    imgs, targets = imgs.cuda(), targets.cuda()
    with torch.cuda.amp.autocast():
        # forward through layers, collect per-layer token embeddings
        layer_feats, attn_stats = backbone.forward_collect(imgs)  # list of [B, T, d]
        # exploration router produces sampling probs per token-layer
        expl_logits = exploration_router(layer_feats)  # per layer
        # sample probing tokens
        sampled = sample_tokens_layers(expl_logits, per_img=16, layers=L)
        dr_terms = []
        for (b, l, t_idx, d_depth) in sampled:
            # shallow stop
            shallow_repr = prefix_repr(layer_feats[b], l)
            logits_sh, log_sigma_sh = shallow_head(shallow_repr)
            loss_sh = ce_with_uncertainty(logits_sh, log_sigma_sh, targets[b])
            # deeper path for selected token only (pack tokens)
            pack = pack_token(layer_feats[b], l, t_idx)
            deeper_repr = run_deeper_blocks(pack, start=l, steps=d_depth)
            logits_dp, log_sigma_dp = deep_head(deeper_repr)
            loss_dp = ce_with_uncertainty(logits_dp, log_sigma_dp, targets[b])
            delta_cf = (loss_sh - loss_dp).detach()
            # critic input
            token_feat = layer_feats[l][b, t_idx]
            aux = build_aux(attn_stats[l][b, t_idx], shallow_logits=logits_sh.detach())
            mu, log_sigma = critic(token_feat.unsqueeze(0), torch.tensor([l], device='cuda'), aux.unsqueeze(0))
            # importance weight
            pi_e = expl_prob(expl_logits[l][b, t_idx])
            pi_t = target_prob(router_scores[l][b, t_idx])
            w = (pi_t / (pi_e + 1e-6)).clamp(max=10.0)
            # doubly-robust loss terms
            dr = w * ((delta_cf - mu.squeeze())**2 / (2*torch.exp(2*log_sigma.squeeze())) + 0.5*log_sigma)
            dr_terms.append(dr)
        L_DR = torch.stack(dr_terms).mean() if dr_terms else torch.tensor(0., device='cuda')
        # TD consistency (compute V(i,l+Δl) via critic forward on next-layer reprs)
        L_TD = td_consistency(critic, layer_feats, sampled)
        # Router and task losses
        V_tilde = get_value_scores_for_all_tokens(critic, layer_feats, attn_stats)
        score = V_tilde - budget_ctrl.lmbda * cost_per_token
        soft_weights = soft_router(tokens=layer_feats, value_signal=score)
        logits_sh, log_sigma_sh = shallow_head_from_prefix(layer_feats)
        logits_dp, log_sigma_dp = deep_head_from_selected(layer_feats, soft_weights)
        logits_fused = fuse_uncertainty(logits_sh, log_sigma_sh, logits_dp, log_sigma_dp)
        L_task = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits_fused, targets)
        L = L_task + L_DR + 0.1 * L_TD + 1e-3 * router_entropy(soft_weights)
    scaler.scale(L).backward(); scaler.step(optim); scaler.update()
    # Budget dual ascent using measured latency (optionally periodic)
    lat_ms = measure_latency_last_iter()
    budget_ctrl.update(lat_ms)

# Calibrate critic and risk threshold
iso = IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds='clip').fit(mu_val, delta_cf_val)
V_tilde_val = iso.predict(mu_val) - eta * sigma_val
theta = calibrator.pick_theta_expectation(delta_hat_val, delta_true_val, epsilon=0.5)


Experiment 2 — Dense segmentation with shallow–deep complementarity and fairness
Goals/Hypotheses
- H3: Complementary shallow–deep heads with uncertainty fusion mitigate quality cliffs and improve Pareto vs single-head routing.
- H4: Fairness/coverage constraints reduce spatial starvation and improve OOD stability.

Dataset and preprocessing
- Dataset: Cityscapes with fine annotations (train 2975, val 500). Optional Cityscapes-C (synthetic corruptions) for OOD evaluation.
- Preprocessing: Random scaling [0.5, 2.0], random crops 768×768 (or 512×1024), horizontal flips, color jitter; evaluation uses single-scale 1024×2048 or 768×1536 resize depending on GPU.

Backbone and tokens
- Models: SegFormer-B2 encoder (MiT-B2) + decoder head from mmlab/mmsegmentation or timm-ported SegFormer; or ViT-S + lightweight FPN head.
- Tokens: spatial tokens at 1/4 or 1/8 stride (MiT tokens). CLS not used.
- Routing layers: select 3–4 routing points across the encoder stages (e.g., after stage blocks where token resolution is stable). Δl ∈ {1,2} for efficiency.

Reference model (Stage 0)
- Train full-depth model to a strong baseline mIoU: AdamW, lr=6e-5, wd=0.01, cosine, 160k iterations with poly LR as in mmseg recipes; mixed precision; SyncBN if multi-GPU. Save the reference.

Twin-Critic for dense tasks (Stage 1)
- Counterfactual Δℓ_cf: Per sampled token (spatial position), compute per-pixel CE averaged over the full image for shallow vs deeper routes. For localized value signals, optionally compute a local window CE around the token (e.g., 16×16 patch) but aggregate to sample-level loss for calibration stability.
- Probing: S=32 tokens/image across routing layers; Δl in {1,2}; same DR+TD training as Exp1. Auxiliary features include spatial gradient magnitude (via Sobel from kornia), boundary proximity (distance transform from segmentation predictions), and local attention entropy.
- Calibration: Isotonic μ→Δℓ_cf per layer or shared map across layers; Ṽ = iso(μ) − η σ.

Complementary heads and uncertainty fusion (Stage 2)
- Subbands: Use kornia.filters.laplacian_pyramid to decompose logits/features. Shallow head predicts low-frequency logits (apply avg pool or low-pass filter). Deep head predicts residual high-frequency logits.
- Uncertainty fusion: Each head outputs per-pixel logits and log σ. Fuse per class c: logits_fused_c = (L_sh,c/σ_sh^2 + L_dp,c/σ_dp^2) / (1/σ_sh^2 + 1/σ_dp^2). Train with pixel-wise CE on fused logits. Optionally include auxiliary loss on low- and high-frequency components.
- Complementarity regularizers:
  • HSIC: encourage low dependence between shallow features and deep residual features. Use a fast linear-kernel HSIC (covariance-based) to avoid heavy kernels. Add λ_hsic=1e-3.
  • Spectral orthogonalization: Project deep residual features onto the orthogonal complement of shallow features via a learned bottleneck: minimize ||Proj_shallow(deep_resid)||^2 with λ_spec=1e-3.
- Fairness/coverage constraints:
  • Grid quotas: Divide the spatial token grid into 8×8 cells; enforce at least q_min and at most q_max tokens per cell per layer (e.g., q_min=1, q_max=4), implemented via soft penalties when router assigns too much mass to any single cell. This avoids starvation and improves stability under shift.
  • Diversity regularizer: Optionally use a DPP-style determinant penalty over selected token embeddings to promote diversity; use dppy or a simplified cosine-diversity penalty to avoid heavy ops.
- Router objective: Same value-minus-cost per token s(i,l) = Ṽ(i,l) − λ c_l; train with soft top-k and fairness penalties.

Risk calibration (Stage 3)
- Sample-level Δℓ̂: Sum token-level Ṽ of routed tokens; for segmentation, compute Δℓ as the difference in mean pixel-wise CE between exit and full-depth. Fit isotonic Δℓ̂→Δℓ and pick θ for ε in mIoU drop translated to CE units (empirically calibrate CE threshold corresponding to ε mIoU drop using a validation sweep or use Δℓ as CE difference directly and report mIoU changes separately).

Evaluation and metrics
- Pareto: Latency (ms) or GFLOPs vs mIoU on Cityscapes val; plot and compute AUP. Compare: MoD-RCC (ours), proxy router, uniform, and FYS early exit (global) baselines.
- Risk satisfaction: For ε ∈ {0.01, 0.02, 0.05} CE increase equivalents, report E[Δℓ] and P(Δℓ > ε) on clean val and Cityscapes-C. Also report mIoU drops at the same operating points.
- Spatial coverage: Measure fraction of images with any empty 8×8 cell, Gini coefficient of per-cell routed tokens, JS divergence between routing heatmap and uniform, and correlation with semantic boundaries (compute boundary map via Canny or ground-truth edges).
- Visualizations: Overlay routed tokens on images and predicted boundaries; show stability across corruptions.
- Ablations: remove complementarity penalties; remove uncertainty fusion (simple addition); remove fairness constraints; swap Ṽ for proxy scores.

Hyperparameters and runtime
- Training: Stage 1 (critic) ~40k iters, Stage 2 (router+heads) ~80k iters on a single A6000/3090 24GB with mixed precision. Batch size 4–8 depending on resolution.
- Router k_l: choose 10–30% of tokens per layer; tune via budget controller for target latencies.
- Fairness: q_min=1, q_max=4 per cell; entropy reg 1e-3.

Example snippets (abbreviated)

# Build Laplacian decomposition and uncertainty fusion
import kornia as K
low_sh, high_sh = K.filters.GaussianBlur2d((5,5), (1.5,1.5))(feat_sh).chunk(2, dim=1)
logits_sh, log_sigma_sh = shallow_head(low_sh)
logits_dp_res, log_sigma_dp = deep_head(high_dp)
logits_dp = logits_dp_res  # residual
# fuse
w_sh = torch.exp(-2*log_sigma_sh)
w_dp = torch.exp(-2*log_sigma_dp)
logits_fused = (w_sh*logits_sh + w_dp*logits_dp) / (w_sh + w_dp + 1e-6)
loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits_fused, seg_targets)
# fairness penalty
grid_counts = count_tokens_per_cell(router_weights, H_tokens, W_tokens, grid=8)
penalty = ((grid_counts - grid_counts.clamp(q_min, q_max))**2).mean()
loss = loss + 1e-3 * penalty


Experiment 3 — Online distribution-free risk control under shift (streaming ImageNet-C/Cityscapes-C)
Goals/Hypotheses
- H5: Post-hoc isotonic + WSR high-prob calibrator maintains risk caps under distribution shift with anytime-valid updates, unlike static thresholds or proxy routers.

Setup
- Streamed data: Concatenate all corruption types at severities 1→5 in deterministic order to simulate gradual and abrupt distribution shifts. For Cityscapes-C, apply corruptions on the fly. Batch size 64; process sequentially.
- Model: Use MoD-RCC models from Exp1/2 with trained critic, router, and initial calibrators (θ0 from clean validation).
- Modes compared: (a) Static θ from clean calibration; (b) Online WSR-based threshold updates; (c) Proxy router + CRC-like static calibration; (d) FYS early exit with static CRC threshold.

WSR-based online control
- Observations per sample t: Δℓ̂_t (predicted degradation), Δℓ_t (true realized degradation vs full-depth). Define violation indicator Z_t = 1{Δℓ_t > ε}. The goal is to adapt θ_t such that P(Z_t=1) ≤ δ over time with anytime-valid guarantees.
- Anytime-valid update: Maintain a nonnegative supermartingale M_t associated with the Bernoulli process of violations under the null that the violation probability ≤ δ (per Waudby-Smith & Ramdas). One practical update:
  • Use the mixture supermartingale S_t = Π_{s=1}^t (1 − η_{s-1} + η_{s-1} (Z_s/δ)), with learning rate η_t in [0,1]. If S_t exceeds 1/α, the null is violated at level α. We invert this logic to shrink or grow θ_t to keep S_t below threshold.
  • Implementation heuristic: After each batch, compute the batch violation rate v̂. If upper confidence bound UCB(v̂) (e.g., Clopper–Pearson at 1−α) > δ, increase θ_t by small step Δθ to be more conservative; else decrease θ_t slightly to save compute. Keep θ_t monotone w.r.t. recent empirical risk via isotonic smoothing over time.
- Asynchronous Δℓ measurement: To avoid doubling latency, compute full-depth outputs asynchronously on a separate GPU/CPU worker (or reuse precomputed reference logits for ImageNet-C if available). Maintain a short delay buffer; threshold updates can lag by one batch without violating anytime validity over realized history.

Streaming procedure (per batch)
- Compute Δℓ̂ for each sample using aggregated token values; decide exit if Δℓ̂ ≥ θ_t else use full-depth. Produce outputs and log per-sample latency.
- When full-depth references are available (for exit or for a subsample if deferring is costly), compute Δℓ and mark violation.
- Update WSR statistic (or simply compute a nonparametric UCB for Bernoulli violations) and adjust θ_t accordingly.

Metrics
- Guarantee tracking: Plot running violation rate and its Clopper–Pearson 95% CI vs time; report sup_t (1/t) Σ_{s≤t} 1{Δℓ_s>ε}. Should stay below δ for WSR mode.
- Latency and regret: Report average latency and average degradation Δℓ per mode; compute regret relative to an oracle static θ tuned per corruption severity (upper bound).
- Adaptation: Time-to-recover after corruption switches (samples needed to bring the violation CI below δ again).
- Complexity: Incremental overhead from WSR updates (<1% runtime) and async reference computation.

Example streaming loop (abbreviated)

from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint
viol_sum = 0; n_seen = 0; theta = theta0
for batch in stream_loader:
    out, delta_hat = mod_rcc.predict(batch['x'].cuda(), budget=B, epsilon=None)
    # decision
    use_exit = (delta_hat >= theta)
    # async or cached full-depth refs yield delta_true later; here assume immediate for simplicity
    delta_true = measure_degradation(batch, out, ref_model)
    z = (delta_true > epsilon).float()
    viol_sum += z.sum().item(); n_seen += len(z)
    lo, hi = proportion_confint(viol_sum, n_seen, method='beta')
    # adjust theta conservatively if hi > δ
    if hi > delta_target:
        theta += step_up
    else:
        theta = max(theta_min, theta - step_down)
    log_stats(theta=theta, viol_rate=viol_sum/n_seen, ci=(lo,hi))


Reliability and engineering practices (applies to all experiments)
- Latency tables: Build c_l as a function of routed token count using torch.utils.benchmark.Timer with warmup and repeats. Fit a smooth spline (scipy.interpolate.UnivariateSpline) for quick lookup.
- Token packing: Use torch.index_select for packed token embedding arrays and inverse scatter via index_copy_. Preallocate with maximum k_l per layer to avoid realloc.
- Attention kernels: Use xformers.ops.memory_efficient_attention or flash_attn (FlashAttention2) to accelerate transformer blocks; ensure these support variable token counts.
- Mixed precision: Use GradScaler; keep critic loss in float32 to stabilize heteroscedastic regression.
- Logging and reproducibility: Log seed, git commit, configs (Hydra/OmegaConf), per-layer routing histograms, per-sample Δℓ̂ and Δℓ to Parquet for post-hoc analyses.
- Sanity checks: At start, verify that (a) Ṽ distributions are centered near observed Δℓ_cf; (b) routing fraction matches budget; (c) calibration holds on validation via reliability diagrams (Δℓ̂ vs Δℓ bins with sklearn.calibration.calibration_curve analog for regression).

Ablation and sensitivity studies
- Critic variants: TD-only vs DR; no isotonic vs isotonic; with/without uncertainty (σ).
- Router: soft vs hard straight-through; entropy reg strength; fairness constraints on/off.
- Costs: FLOPs-based vs latency-based c_l; device-specific tables (e.g., A100 vs 3090) to show portability.
- Complementarity: Remove HSIC and spectral penalties; compare to single-head fusion; measure change in boundary mIoU.
- Calibration: Static θ vs WSR online; varying ε and δ.

Expected outcomes
- Exp1: MoD-RCC achieves superior Pareto (latency vs top-1) vs proxy and uniform routing; satisfies expectation and HP risk caps; shows higher MCV ranking correlation and lower value leakage.
- Exp2: Complementarity and fairness smooth quality degradation and improve mIoU at fixed latency; better OOD robustness on Cityscapes-C with online adaptation.
- Exp3: WSR-based updates maintain violation rates below δ across corruption streams with minimal extra latency; static calibrations degrade under shift.

Notes on limits and mitigations
- Counterfactual probing cost: Keep S small (8–32 tokens/image) and reuse shared forward states to limit overhead; stagger layers across steps. Use gradient checkpointing.
- Importance-weight variance: Clip weights; maintain exploration close to target policy; use doubly-robust combination with TD to reduce variance.
- Calibration drift: Recalibrate isotonic periodically or use online isotonic (PAV incremental approximations) if severe drift is observed.

Checklist for release and reproducibility
- Provide configs for all experiments (Hydra), scripts for building latency tables, unit tests for token packing and calibration, and a minimal Colab/demo with ImageNet-100.
- Provide ablation sweeps scripts and plotting utilities for Pareto and reliability curves.
experimental_design.experiment_code:
# MoD-RCC — Risk-Controlled, Value-Aware Mixture-of-Depths for Token-Wise Dynamic Computation
# Compact PyTorch reference implementation for three experiments (classification, segmentation, streaming risk control).
# This script is designed to run quickly on CPU/GPU for functional testing using synthetic data.
#
# Key features implemented:
# - TwinCriticMCV (heteroscedastic regression for marginal compute value per token)
# - SoftTopKRouter (soft training / hard inference top-k routing)
# - BudgetController (dual variable for latency/compute budget)
# - Complementary shallow/deep heads with uncertainty fusion
# - RiskCalibrator (isotonic regression via PAV and threshold selection)
# - Dynamic token routing at selected layers; token packing simulated via masking
# - Multiple data patterns (clean vs corrupted) to demonstrate robustness
# - Plots saved as PDF files (for paper-ready figures)
# - Print statements showing experiment progress and results
# - A quick test function to validate functionality in seconds
#
# Necessary Python libraries to run the experiments:
# - torch (PyTorch)
# - torchvision
# - numpy
# - matplotlib
# - tqdm (optional but used)
# - seaborn (optional; not required here)
#
# Note: Deep learning framework is PyTorch exclusively; other utilities are standard Python/NumPy/Matplotlib.

import math
import time
import random
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Tuple, Optional, Dict

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from tqdm import tqdm

# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Utilities
# ------------------------------------------------------------

def set_seed(seed: int = 42):
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False


def accuracy_top1(logits: torch.Tensor, targets: torch.Tensor) -> float:
    pred = logits.argmax(dim=-1)
    return (pred == targets).float().mean().item()


def confusion_matrix(pred: np.ndarray, target: np.ndarray, num_classes: int) -> np.ndarray:
    cm = np.zeros((num_classes, num_classes), dtype=np.int64)
    for p, t in zip(pred, target):
        if 0 <= t < num_classes and 0 <= p < num_classes:
            cm[t, p] += 1
    return cm


def mIoU_from_confmat(cm: np.ndarray) -> float:
    intersection = np.diag(cm)
    ground_truth_set = cm.sum(axis=1)
    predicted_set = cm.sum(axis=0)
    union = ground_truth_set + predicted_set - intersection
    iou = intersection / np.maximum(union, 1)
    return float(np.nanmean(iou))


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Synthetic Datasets (Classification and Segmentation)
# ------------------------------------------------------------

class SyntheticShapesClassification(Dataset):
    """Simple 10-class synthetic classification dataset.
    - Image: 32x32 RGB
    - Label: quadrant/shape/color combination to create meaningful patterns
    - Two patterns: clean (sharp shapes) and corrupted (Gaussian noise/blur)
    """
    def __init__(self, n: int = 1000, img_size: int = 32, n_classes: int = 10, pattern: str = 'clean'):
        self.n = n
        self.img_size = img_size
        self.n_classes = n_classes
        self.pattern = pattern
        self.images, self.labels = self._generate()

    def _generate(self) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        H = W = self.img_size
        imgs = torch.zeros((self.n, 3, H, W), dtype=torch.float32)
        labels = torch.zeros((self.n,), dtype=torch.long)
        for i in range(self.n):
            img = torch.zeros(3, H, W)
            # Choose class 0..9
            c = torch.randint(low=0, high=self.n_classes, size=(1,)).item()
            # Encode class into shape + color + position
            shape = 'square' if c % 2 == 0 else 'circle'
            color_channel = c % 3
            pos_id = (c // 3) % 3  # 0,1,2
            size = H // 4
            # Position mapping
            if pos_id == 0:
                cx, cy = H // 4, W // 4
            elif pos_id == 1:
                cx, cy = H // 2, W // 2
            else:
                cx, cy = 3 * H // 4, 3 * W // 4
            y, x = torch.meshgrid(torch.arange(H), torch.arange(W), indexing='ij')
            if shape == 'square':
                mask = (x >= cx - size) & (x <= cx + size) & (y >= cy - size) & (y <= cy + size)
            else:
                mask = ((x - cx) ** 2 + (y - cy) ** 2) <= (size ** 2)
            img[color_channel, mask] = 1.0
            # Add small background noise
            img += 0.05 * torch.randn_like(img)
            img = torch.clamp(img, 0.0, 1.0)
            # Corruption patterns
            if self.pattern == 'corrupt':
                # Gaussian blur + stronger noise
                img = F.avg_pool2d(img.unsqueeze(0), kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1).squeeze(0)
                img += 0.15 * torch.randn_like(img)
                img = torch.clamp(img, 0.0, 1.0)
            imgs[i] = img
            labels[i] = c
        return imgs, labels

    def __len__(self):
        return self.n

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return self.images[idx], self.labels[idx]


class SyntheticSegmentation(Dataset):
    """Toy segmentation dataset (3 classes) on 32x32 images with shapes.
    pattern='clean' or 'corrupt' to add noise/blur."""
    def __init__(self, n: int = 200, img_size: int = 32, n_classes: int = 3, pattern: str = 'clean'):
        self.n = n
        self.img_size = img_size
        self.n_classes = n_classes
        self.pattern = pattern
        self.images, self.labels = self._generate()

    def _generate(self) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        H = W = self.img_size
        imgs = torch.zeros((self.n, 3, H, W), dtype=torch.float32)
        labels = torch.zeros((self.n, H, W), dtype=torch.long)
        for i in range(self.n):
            img = torch.zeros(3, H, W)
            lab = torch.zeros(H, W, dtype=torch.long)
            # Background class 0
            # Add a rectangle (class 1) and a circle (class 2)
            size = H // 5
            cx1, cy1 = H // 3, W // 3
            cx2, cy2 = 2 * H // 3, 2 * W // 3
            y, x = torch.meshgrid(torch.arange(H), torch.arange(W), indexing='ij')
            rect = (x >= cx1 - size) & (x <= cx1 + size) & (y >= cy1 - size) & (y <= cy1 + size)
            circ = ((x - cx2) ** 2 + (y - cy2) ** 2) <= (size ** 2)
            lab[rect] = 1
            lab[circ] = 2
            img[0, rect] = 0.8
            img[1, circ] = 0.8
            img += 0.05 * torch.randn_like(img)
            img = torch.clamp(img, 0.0, 1.0)
            if self.pattern == 'corrupt':
                img = F.avg_pool2d(img.unsqueeze(0), kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1).squeeze(0)
                img += 0.15 * torch.randn_like(img)
                img = torch.clamp(img, 0.0, 1.0)
            imgs[i] = img
            labels[i] = lab
        return imgs, labels

    def __len__(self):
        return self.n

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return self.images[idx], self.labels[idx]


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Core MoD-RCC modules
# ------------------------------------------------------------

class PatchEmbed(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_chans=3, embed_dim=64, patch_size=4, img_size=32):
        super().__init__()
        self.patch_size = patch_size
        self.img_size = img_size
        self.proj = nn.Conv2d(in_chans, embed_dim, kernel_size=patch_size, stride=patch_size)
        self.num_patches = (img_size // patch_size) * (img_size // patch_size)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.proj(x)  # [B, C, H/ps, W/ps]
        B, C, H, W = x.shape
        x = x.flatten(2).transpose(1, 2)  # [B, N, C]
        return x, (H, W)


class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim: int, num_heads: int, mlp_ratio: float = 4.0, attn_drop=0.0, drop=0.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        self.qkv = nn.Linear(dim, dim * 3)
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = dim // num_heads
        self.scale = self.head_dim ** -0.5
        self.attn_drop = nn.Dropout(attn_drop)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(dim, dim)
        self.proj_drop = nn.Dropout(drop)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        hidden = int(dim * mlp_ratio)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(dim, hidden), nn.GELU(), nn.Linear(hidden, dim), nn.Dropout(drop)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        B, N, C = x.shape
        h = self.norm1(x)
        qkv = self.qkv(h).reshape(B, N, 3, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).permute(2, 0, 3, 1, 4)
        q, k, v = qkv[0], qkv[1], qkv[2]  # [B, heads, N, head_dim]
        attn = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) * self.scale
        attn = attn.softmax(dim=-1)
        attn = self.attn_drop(attn)
        out = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, N, C)
        out = self.proj(out)
        out = self.proj_drop(out)
        x = x + out
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x, attn  # return attn for aux stats


class MiniViT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, img_size=32, patch_size=4, in_chans=3, embed_dim=64, depth=6, num_heads=4, num_classes=10, cls_token=True):
        super().__init__()
        self.patch_embed = PatchEmbed(in_chans, embed_dim, patch_size, img_size)
        self.num_patches = self.patch_embed.num_patches
        self.cls_token = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1, 1, embed_dim)) if cls_token else None
        self.pos_embed = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(1, (1 if cls_token else 0) + self.num_patches, embed_dim) * 0.02)
        self.blocks = nn.ModuleList([
            TransformerBlock(embed_dim, num_heads) for _ in range(depth)
        ])
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.num_classes = num_classes
        self.head = nn.Linear(embed_dim, num_classes)
        self.cls_token_used = cls_token

    def forward_collect(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[List[torch.Tensor], List[torch.Tensor]]:
        # Returns per-layer token embeddings and attention stats
        B = x.size(0)
        tokens, spatial_hw = self.patch_embed(x)  # [B, N, C]
        if self.cls_token_used:
            cls_tok = self.cls_token.expand(B, -1, -1)  # [B, 1, C]
            tokens = torch.cat([cls_tok, tokens], dim=1)
        tokens = tokens + self.pos_embed[:, :tokens.size(1), :]
        feats = []
        attns = []
        h = tokens
        for blk in self.blocks:
            h, attn = blk(h)
            feats.append(h)
            attns.append(attn)
        h = self.norm(h)
        feats[-1] = h
        return feats, attns  # list of [B, N, C]

    def forward_head(self, tokens: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # tokens: [B, N, C]; use CLS if present else mean pool
        if self.cls_token_used:
            cls = tokens[:, 0]
        else:
            cls = tokens.mean(dim=1)
        logits = self.head(cls)
        return logits

    def forward(self, x):
        feats, _ = self.forward_collect(x)
        return self.forward_head(feats[-1])


class UncertaintyHead(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim: int, num_classes: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.logits = nn.Linear(dim, num_classes)
        self.log_sigma = nn.Linear(dim, 1)

    def forward(self, tokens: torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        # tokens: [B, N, C]; use CLS or mean pool before head
        if tokens.size(1) > 1:
            pooled = tokens[:, 0]
        else:
            pooled = tokens.squeeze(1)
        return self.logits(pooled), self.log_sigma(pooled).clamp(-3, 3)


class TwinCriticMCV(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, n_layers: int, aux_dim: int = 16, hidden: int = 128):
        super().__init__()
        self.layer_embed = nn.Embedding(n_layers, 16)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model + 16 + aux_dim, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU()
        )
        self.head_mu = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)
        self.head_logsigma = nn.Linear(hidden, 1)

    def forward(self, token_embed: torch.Tensor, layer_id: torch.Tensor, aux: torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        # token_embed: [T, d]; layer_id: [T]; aux: [T, aux_dim]
        lid = self.layer_embed(layer_id)
        h = torch.cat([token_embed, lid, aux], dim=-1)
        h = self.mlp(h)
        mu = self.head_mu(h).squeeze(-1)
        log_sigma = self.head_logsigma(h).squeeze(-1).clamp(-6, 3)
        return mu, log_sigma


class SoftTopKRouter(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, k_per_layer: int, temperature: float = 0.7, entropy_reg: float = 1e-3):
        super().__init__()
        self.k = k_per_layer
        self.tau = temperature
        self.entropy_reg = entropy_reg

    def forward(self, score: torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        # score: [T] (higher = more compute)
        # Soft top-k via Gumbel-softmax; normalized to sum close to k
        g = -torch.log(-torch.log(torch.rand_like(score)))
        y = (score + g) / self.tau
        w = torch.softmax(y, dim=0)
        w = w * (self.k / (w.sum() + 1e-8))
        entropy = -(w * (w.clamp(min=1e-8).log())).sum()
        return w, entropy

    @staticmethod
    def hard_topk(score: torch.Tensor, k: int) -> torch.Tensor:
        idx = torch.topk(score, k=k, dim=0).indices
        mask = torch.zeros_like(score, dtype=torch.bool)
        mask[idx] = True
        return mask


class BudgetController:
    def __init__(self, target_cost: float = 1.0, rho: float = 1e-3, device: torch.device = torch.device('cpu')):
        self.lmbda = torch.tensor(0.0, device=device)
        self.target = target_cost
        self.rho = rho

    def update(self, measured_cost: float) -> float:
        with torch.no_grad():
            self.lmbda = (self.lmbda + self.rho * (measured_cost - self.target)).clamp(min=0.0)
        return float(self.lmbda.item())


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Isotonic Regression (PAV) for Risk Calibration
# ------------------------------------------------------------

class IsotonicCalibrator:
    """Simple isotonic regression (non-decreasing) using Pool Adjacent Violators (PAV).
    Fits y on x and can predict for new x by linear interpolation.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.x_: Optional[np.ndarray] = None
        self.y_: Optional[np.ndarray] = None

    def fit(self, x: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray):
        order = np.argsort(x)
        x = x[order]
        y = y[order]
        # PAV
        y_hat = y.copy().astype(np.float64)
        w = np.ones_like(y_hat)
        i = 0
        while i < len(y_hat) - 1:
            if y_hat[i] <= y_hat[i + 1] + 1e-12:
                i += 1
            else:
                # pool violators
                j = i
                while j >= 0 and y_hat[j] > y_hat[j + 1] + 1e-12:
                    total_w = w[j] + w[j + 1]
                    avg = (w[j] * y_hat[j] + w[j + 1] * y_hat[j + 1]) / total_w
                    y_hat[j] = avg
                    y_hat[j + 1] = avg
                    w[j] = total_w
                    # shift back if needed
                    j -= 1
                i += 1
        self.x_ = x
        self.y_ = y_hat

    def predict(self, xq: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        assert self.x_ is not None and self.y_ is not None
        x = self.x_; y = self.y_
        # piecewise constant/linear interpolation
        idx = np.searchsorted(x, xq, side='left')
        idx = np.clip(idx, 1, len(x) - 1)
        x0 = x[idx - 1]; x1 = x[idx]
        y0 = y[idx - 1]; y1 = y[idx]
        t = np.where((x1 - x0) > 0, (xq - x0) / (x1 - x0), 0.0)
        return (1 - t) * y0 + t * y1


class RiskCalibrator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.iso = IsotonicCalibrator()
        self.theta: Optional[float] = None

    def fit(self, delta_hat: np.ndarray, delta_true: np.ndarray):
        self.iso.fit(delta_hat, delta_true)

    def map(self, delta_hat: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        return self.iso.predict(delta_hat)

    def pick_theta_expectation(self, delta_hat: np.ndarray, delta_true: np.ndarray, epsilon: float) -> float:
        # Choose minimal threshold so mean degradation among accepted <= epsilon
        qs = np.linspace(0.0, 1.0, 51)
        ths = np.quantile(delta_hat, qs)
        best = ths[0]
        for th in ths:
            mask = delta_hat >= th
            if mask.sum() == 0:
                continue
            mean_deg = float(delta_true[mask].mean())
            if mean_deg <= epsilon:
                best = th
                break
        self.theta = float(best)
        return float(best)


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Router + Heads glue utilities
# ------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class RoutingConfig:
    routing_layers: List[int]  # layers where decisions are made (0-indexed)
    k_per_layer: int           # number of tokens to route at each routing layer (excluding CLS)


def build_aux_features(tokens: torch.Tensor, attn: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    # tokens [B, N, C]; attn [B, H, N, N]
    # Simple aux: token L2 norm, attention entropy, positional index fraction
    B, N, C = tokens.shape
    Hh = attn.size(1)
    with torch.no_grad():
        tnorm = tokens.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True)  # [B, N, 1]
        A = attn.mean(dim=1)  # [B, N, N]
        A = A / (A.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)
        entropy = -(A * (A.clamp(min=1e-8).log())).sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True) / math.log(max(N, 2))  # [B, N, 1]
        posfrac = torch.linspace(0, 1, N, device=tokens.device).view(1, N, 1).expand(B, N, 1)
    aux = torch.cat([tnorm, entropy, posfrac, torch.zeros(B, N, 13, device=tokens.device)], dim=-1)
    return aux  # [B, N, 16]


def heteroscedastic_mse(mu: torch.Tensor, log_sigma: torch.Tensor, target: torch.Tensor, weight: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None) -> torch.Tensor:
    var = torch.exp(2.0 * log_sigma)
    loss = 0.5 * ((target - mu) ** 2) / (var + 1e-8) + 0.5 * torch.log(var + 1e-8)
    if weight is not None:
        loss = loss * weight
    return loss.mean()


def fuse_uncertainty(logits_sh: torch.Tensor, log_sigma_sh: torch.Tensor,
                     logits_dp: torch.Tensor, log_sigma_dp: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    w_sh = torch.exp(-2.0 * log_sigma_sh).clamp(1e-4, 1e4)
    w_dp = torch.exp(-2.0 * log_sigma_dp).clamp(1e-4, 1e4)
    # Broadcast to logits shape
    while w_sh.dim() < logits_sh.dim():
        w_sh = w_sh.unsqueeze(-1)
    while w_dp.dim() < logits_dp.dim():
        w_dp = w_dp.unsqueeze(-1)
    fused = (w_sh * logits_sh + w_dp * logits_dp) / (w_sh + w_dp + 1e-8)
    return fused


# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Experiment 1: Classification with Value-aware MoD + Risk Control
# ------------------------------------------------------------

def experiment1_classification(device: torch.device = torch.device('cpu')):
    print("\n=== Experiment 1: Classification (MoD-RCC) ===")
    set_seed(123)

    # Data (two patterns: clean vs corrupt)
    train_ds = SyntheticShapesClassification(n=800, pattern='clean')
    val_clean_ds = SyntheticShapesClassification(n=200, pattern='clean')
    val_corrupt_ds = SyntheticShapesClassification(n=200, pattern='corrupt')
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=True)
    val_clean_loader = DataLoader(val_clean_ds, batch_size=64)
    val_corrupt_loader = DataLoader(val_corrupt_ds, batch_size=64)

    # Model setup
    img_size = 32
    num_classes = 10
    model = MiniViT(img_size=img_size, patch_size=4, embed_dim=64, depth=6, num_heads=4, num_classes=num_classes, cls_token=True).to(device)
    optim_ref = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-3, weight_decay=1e-2)

    # Stage 0: Train reference full-depth model (few epochs for speed)
    print("[Stage 0] Training full-depth reference model (brief)...")
    epochs_ref = 2
    loss_hist = []
    for ep in range(epochs_ref):
        model.train()
        ep_loss = 0.0
        for x, y in train_loader:
            x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
            optim_ref.zero_grad()
            logits = model(x)
            loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, y)
            loss.backward()
            optim_ref.step()
            ep_loss += loss.item() * x.size(0)
        ep_loss /= len(train_loader.dataset)
        loss_hist.append(ep_loss)
        # Eval quick
        model.eval()
        acc_clean = 0.0
        with torch.no_grad():
            for x, y in val_clean_loader:
                x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
                logits = model(x)
                acc_clean += accuracy_top1(logits, y) * x.size(0)
        acc_clean /= len(val_clean_loader.dataset)
        print(f"  Epoch {ep+1}/{epochs_ref} - train loss {ep_loss:.4f} - val(clean) acc {acc_clean*100:.2f}%")

    # Save training loss plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(4,3))
    plt.plot(loss_hist, label='train_loss')
    plt.xlabel('epoch')
    plt.ylabel('loss')
    plt.title('Reference training loss')
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig('training_loss_baseline.pdf', bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close()

    # Create shallow & deep heads + critic + router + budget
    shallow_head = UncertaintyHead(dim=64, num_classes=num_classes).to(device)
    deep_head = UncertaintyHead(dim=64, num_classes=num_classes).to(device)
    critic = TwinCriticMCV(d_model=64, n_layers=6, aux_dim=16, hidden=128).to(device)
    router_cfg = RoutingConfig(routing_layers=[2, 4], k_per_layer=32)  # route 32 tokens (excluding CLS) at these layers
    router = SoftTopKRouter(k_per_layer=router_cfg.k_per_layer, temperature=0.7, entropy_reg=1e-3)
    budget = BudgetController(target_cost=1.0, rho=1e-2, device=device)

    # Optimizers for Stage 1 (critic) and Stage 2 (router+heads)
    optim_critic = torch.optim.AdamW(critic.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1e-4)
    optim_heads = torch.optim.AdamW(list(shallow_head.parameters()) + list(deep_head.parameters()), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1e-4)

    # Stage 1: Train Twin-Critic with counterfactual probing (approximate) on small steps
    print("[Stage 1] Training Twin-Critic (approximate counterfactual labels)")
    model.eval()  # freeze backbone for speed in this demo
    steps = 60
    critic_losses = []
    for it, (x, y) in enumerate(tqdm(train_loader, total=min(steps, len(train_loader)), desc='Critic train')):
        if it >= steps: break
        x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            feats, attns = model.forward_collect(x)
